topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the apartment had become a heavy, suffocating thing. Every time Liam retreated to his home office, the faint, rhythmic scratching sound from behind the closed door would send a cold prickle down my spine. I started noticing the way he held himself—a subtle tension in his shoulders, a quick glance over his shoulder before he settled back down. One Tuesday evening, while I was making tea, I overheard him muttering, "Yes, the betrayal in Chapter Seven needs more nuance." My hand froze mid-air, the porcelain cup trembling slightly in my grasp. I walked to the door, my knuckles hovering near the wood. When I knocked softly, he jumped, startled, and quickly shut the laptop, his face flushing a deep crimson. "Just... working late," he mumbled, his voice too bright, too hurried. The air between us felt suddenly charged, brittle.

[story 2]

I found the evidence tucked beneath a stack of architectural blueprints: a heavily annotated manuscript. The title page, written in his elegant, looping script, was simply "The Observer." My breath hitched as I traced the first few lines. They weren't abstract; they were intensely personal, detailing the exact shade of my eyes during a difficult conversation last month, the precise way I tucked my hair behind my ear when I was concentrating. A knot tightened painfully in my chest, a slow, sickening realization blooming in my gut. I picked up a page, the ink seeming to burn under my fingertips. When he walked in, whistling tunelessly, I didn't look up immediately. I simply held the paper, my gaze fixed on a spot just past his shoulder, my jaw tight. "That's... a lot of detail," I managed, my voice sounding unnaturally level. He stopped dead, his whistling dying in his throat. The sudden stillness in the room was deafening.

[story 3]

It began with misplaced files. I was clearing out the storage closet, and amidst old tax documents, I stumbled upon a leather-bound journal. The handwriting inside was unmistakably his, but the tone shifted—it was intimate, yearning, and overwhelmingly focused on my daily routines, my unspoken anxieties, the tiny, unguarded moments I never realized I was broadcasting. A wave of profound unease washed over me, making my skin feel clammy. I spent the next hour flipping through the pages, the words a relentless spotlight on my private life. When he came home later, smelling faintly of sawdust, I was sitting on the sofa, pretending to read a magazine, but my eyes kept darting toward the door. "Rough day?" he asked, his tone laced with a practiced ease that felt entirely manufactured. I managed a weak smile, the effort costing me dearly. "Just thinking about… the future," I replied, the words tasting like ash in my mouth.
